1: Welcome. This is a calm walkthrough about the two of you as a couple when you become parents. The relationship is often put to the test in the first stretch, and that is common. We take it at your own pace and without finger-wagging.
2: We will talk about going from a couple to parents, about sleep deprivation and patience, about sharing the responsibility, and about talking to each other and finding your way back to closeness. We also cover time for yourself and time together, conflicts, each other's wellbeing, and when you can seek support. Stop and talk to each other whenever you like.
3: You are still you, but everything around you changes all at once. Time shrinks, the roles are redrawn, and the feelings can swing between great love and great tiredness at the same time. A relationship creaking a little in the first stretch is common, not a sign that something is wrong.
4: When you sleep too little your patience gets shorter, and often most of all towards each other. A sigh or a tone of voice can land harder than it was meant. Put off the big conversations until you are rested, and help each other with sleep by taking turns. Remember that it is the tiredness that is short in tone, not the two of you against each other.
5: Much of the work with a baby is invisible. Planning, remembering, and keeping track is its own work, and the one who carries it all in their head is often the most worn out. Say out loud what needs to be done and divide up whole tasks, not just help out a little. And don't forget to say thank you for the everyday things, because being seen gives you more energy.
6: You don't have time for long conversations now, and you don't need to. Short check-ins like how are you doing go a long way. Assume that you wish each other well, say what you need instead of guessing, and take one thing at a time. Try saying I feel sad when, instead of you always do, so the other one listens more easily.
7: Closeness comes back, both the physical and the emotional, but rarely all at once. Start small with a hug or a hand, and put no pressure on sex. The body heals and desire swings. Remember that closeness is many things, and that being listened to and having the load eased creates closeness too. Don't compare yourselves with others or with how it used to be.
8: You don't need whole evenings together. It is the small, recurring moments that keep you close. Ten minutes to talk through the day, an evening just the two of you now and then, or a long hug goes a long way. Allow each other a break, and accept help when someone you trust can look after the baby for a while.
9: Almost all couples argue more in the first stretch with a baby. Rubbing each other the wrong way sometimes does not mean you are wrong for each other. Think of it as sitting on the same side of the table, with the problem on the other side, not in each other. Pause rather than win, and repair afterwards. A sorry, I was tired heals more than being right.
10: The first stretch can be hard for anyone, and the partner can struggle too, not only the one who gave birth. Ask each other for real how you are, and stay for the answer. Watch out for persistent low mood, worry, or someone withdrawing. Wellbeing is something you can raise with the child health centre (BVC) or the health centre, not just the baby's health.
11: Asking for support is wise, not a failure, and often easier the earlier you do it. Talk to the BVC or the open preschool about everyday life and how you are doing. Seek couples or family counselling if you get stuck in the same argument over and over. And seek help now if someone is really struggling, or if there are threats or violence.
12: You build this together, one day at a time. Be kind to yourselves and to each other, and ask for support early. What questions do you have?
